EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged snowfall produced heavy accumulations over southeast South Dakota, ranging up to over 20 inches in several areas. The snowfall took place from two days before to the day after Christmas. The snowfall was accompanied by increasing north to northwest winds which caused widespread blizzard conditions on Christmas day and the start of the next day. The blizzard added to the very heavy snowfall made almost all travel impossible, and shut down commerce.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow combined with increasing winds to produce widespread blizzard conditions. The snowfall over 3 days varied from 10 to 18 inches. North to northwest winds reached sustained speeds over 30 mph and there were gusts over 40 mph. These winds combined with the heavy snowfall to produce widespread visibilities below a quarter mile from Christmas day to the start of the next day. Most roads were blocked and travel was brought to a standstill, as was the limited holiday commerce.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread rain changed to wet snow over much of southeast south Dakota. Snow accumulations of 1 to 3 inches were reported in an area north of Interstate 90 from Plankinton to De Smet to Brookings during the morning and early afternoon. Several vehicles slid off roads into roadside ditches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to wet snow, with the snow accumulating 1 to 3 inches. Several vehicles slid into roadside ditches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northwest winds, gusting to over 50 mph, combined with existing loose snow cover and 1 to 3 inches of new snow to produce blizzard conditions with widespread visibilities near zero over parts of southeast South Dakota during the day and early evening of January 12th. The very low visibilities were widespread where the greatest loose snow cover was located, and more localized in other areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North to northwest winds sustained at 30 to 40 mph with gusts over 50 mph combined with several inches of loose snow cover, along with 1 to 3 inches of new snow, to produce widespread visibilities of a quarter mile or less in blowing snow. The low visibilities and drifting snow made travel impossible in many areas, and forced schools and businesses to close.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold temperatures and moderate northwest winds combined to lower wind chill readings to 35 to 45 below zero at times from the morning of January 14th to early morning on January 15th. The strongest winds were at the start of the event. While winds decreased slowly on the night of January 14th, actual temperatures also dropped, reaching 20 below to 30 below zero near daybreak on January 15th, about which time winds became light and no longer a major factor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to strong northwest winds and very cold air combined to lower wind chills to 35 to 45 below zero at times. The coldest winds chills were during the morning of January 14th with strong winds, and the night of January 14th to daybreak of January 15th when temperatures were falling and winds were slowly decreasing. The actual temperature dropped to 31 below zero at Brookings on the morning of January 15th after the wind had subsided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow accompanied by winds gusting to over 40 mph produced blizzard conditions over southeast South Dakota away from the eastern border of the state. The blizzard began on the evening of March 30th and continued through the daytime hours of March 31st. Travel was brought to a standstill, with numerous roads closed, including Interstate 90 from Mitchell west. The greatest reported snow accumulation was 16.5 inches near Corsica.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 10 inches, accompanied by northerly winds gusting over 45 mph, produced blizzard conditions that brought travel and commerce to a standstill. Numerous businesses, schools, and roads were closed; and many roads not officially closed were impassable. The city of Huron received 9.5 inches of snow, with a peak wind gust of 46 mph from the north.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northwest winds, gusting to over 50 mph, combined with existing loose snow cover and 1 to 3 inches of new snow to produce blizzard conditions with widespread visibilities near zero over parts of southeast South Dakota during the day and early evening of January 12th. The very low visibilities were widespread where the greatest loose snow cover was located, and more localized in other areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North to northwest winds sustained at 30 to 40 mph with gusts over 50 mph combined with several inches of loose snow cover, along with 1 to 3 inches of new snow, to produce widespread visibilities of a quarter mile or less in blowing snow. The low visibilities and drifting snow made travel impossible in many areas, and forced schools and businesses to close.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail, accompanied in places by damaging winds, destroyed or severely damaged crops in an area up to 10 miles wide from northwestern to southeastern Yankton County. Estimates from the Farm Service Agency were at least 48 thousand acres of corn, soybean, and alfalfa crops that were affected, some of which were totally destroyed. The hail also broke windows in buildings and vehicles, severely dented and even punched holes in numerous vehicles, and damaged siding and roofs of buildings. Property damage was especially great in and near the cities of Yankton and Gayville. Most of about 200 skylights in a Wal-Mart store in Yankton were broken beyond repair. Some of the windows in vehicles were completely broken and removed by the hail.

Rapid snowmelt and ice jamming caused the Little Minnesota River near Peever to rise above flood stage on March 17th. The river rose to 18.63 feet on March 18th before falling back to below flood stage on March 19th. Flood stage at Peever is 17 feet. The Little Minnesota River had a second rise and went above flood stage again on March 23rd. The second crest was a record crest of 20.38 feet ocurring on March 24th. The river then fell below flood stage again on March 26th. Some fields and a few roads were flooded as a result.

Rapid snowmelt and ice jamming caused flooding on the James River from Columbia to Ashton. The James rose above flood stage on March 21st at Columbia and continued to rise downstream at Stratford and Ashton through March 23rd. Flood stage is 13 feet at Columbia and Ashton and 14 feet at Stratford. The river rose to a record 19.56 feet at Columbia on March 27th, nearly 7 feet above flood stage. The James River continued to flood into April. Many roads, pastureland, agricultural land, along with parts of Columbia were flooded by the river.

Flooding along the James River, which had been slowly decreasing, increased slightly again as a result of additional heavy rains. No major new problems were reported, but flooding of farmland and pastureland near the river continued, as did flooding of a few low lying roads. The river resumed a slow fall by late July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor to moderate flooding along the James River continued from June. The flooding continued to affect farmland and pastureland near the river, with flooding of some low lying roads also continuing. The river crested at 2.4 feet above flood stage at Mitchell on July 14th.

A powerful winter storm moved across the region, bringing heavy snow to the area. Snowfall amounts across the northern Black Hills were generally in the two to four foot range. Lighter snow fell across the central and southern Black Hills, with amounts to eight inches. The plains of Fall River County received six to 12 inches of snow. Gusty winds produced blowing and drifting snow, reducing visibilities below a quarter mile at times.

Prolonged snowfall produced heavy accumulations over southeast South Dakota, ranging up to over 20 inches in several areas. The snowfall took place from two days before to the day after Christmas. The snowfall was accompanied by increasing north to northwest winds which caused widespread blizzard conditions on Christmas day and the start of the next day. The blizzard added to the very heavy snowfall made almost all travel impossible, and shut down commerce.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow combined with increasing winds to produce widespread blizzard conditions. The snowfall over 3 days varied from 16 to 20 inches, including a 20 inches in the Sioux Falls area. North to northwest winds reached sustained speeds over 30 mph and there were gusts over 40 mph. These winds combined with the heavy snowfall to produce widespread visibilities below a quarter mile from Christmas day to the start of the next day. Most roads were blocked and travel was brought to a standstill, as was the limited holiday commerce. Part of the roof of a large cattle barn near Baltic collapsed, killing several cattle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved across the Northern and Central Plains, bringing heavy snow and very strong winds. The worst part of the storm impacted the area from Christmas morning through the morning of December 26, when snow and wind gusts to 80 mph produced near zero visibilities. Six inches to over two feet of snow fell across the plains, with the heaviest amounts from northwestern South Dakota southward to the Pine Ridge. Drifts of five to ten feet were reported. Many roads were closed or impassable during the storm, incuding Interstate 90, which was closed across the state for over 48 hours. Some power outages were also reported.

An early fall storm system brought rain and accumulating snowfall to much of western South Dakota. The heaviest snow occurred over the central Black Hills; where Mount Rushmore, Hill City, and Pactola Reservoir received six to eight inches of snow. Four to six inches of snow fell across the northern and southern Black Hills. The heavy, wet snow downed tree limbs and caused power outages.

Snowfall of 6 to 10 inches over extreme southeast South Dakota was accompanied by north to northwest winds sustained at 30 to 40 mph, with some gusts over 50 mph. The combination of snow and strong winds created widespread blizzard conditions with visibilities near zero. The blizzard closed schools and businesses and halted travel. The snowfall began on the morning of December 8th. The winds increased during the evening of the 8th, and became strong enough to cause the blizzard after midnight on December 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New snowfall of 6 to 9 inches was accompanied by north to northwest winds sustained at 30 to 40 mph, with gusts up to 50 mph. These strong winds produced widespread blizzard conditions beginning a little after midnight on December 9th. Schools and many businesses were closed on December 9th, as the snowfall and near zero visibilities made travel impossible that morning. Blizzard conditions abated that afternoon as winds steadily decreased, although hazardous wind chills which began during the blizzard continued that night and into the early morning of December 10th.

The snowfall began on the morning of December 8th. The winds increased during the evening of the 8th, and became strong enough to cause the blizzard after midnight on December 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 1 to 3 inches was accompanied by northwest winds gusting to over 40 mph. This caused areas of visibilities below a half mile in the falling and blowing snow, with drifting snow also slowing travel. Wind chills dropped to around 20 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level low pressure area over the Upper Midwest associated with an intense surface low pressure area brought widespread heavy snow along with low visibilities in blowing snow to Grant and Deuel counties. The storm was a slow mover and produced several rounds of snow over a three-day period. Total snowfall amounts were from 17 to 22 inches. The heavy snow combined with winds of 20 to 35 mph brought widespread blowing and drifting snow. This storm ranked as one of the top three snowiest for the entire state.||Prior to the onset of the storm, the Governor declared a state of emergency for South Dakota. Most of Interstate-29 was closed late on December 24th and not reopened until the morning of December 27th. There were some stranded holiday travelers due to the road closings along with a few accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northwest winds, gusting to over 50 mph, combined with existing loose snow cover and 1 to 3 inches of new snow to produce blizzard conditions with widespread visibilities near zero over parts of southeast South Dakota during the day and early evening of January 12th. The very low visibilities were widespread where the greatest loose snow cover was located, and more localized in other areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North to northwest winds gusting to around 50 mph, existing snow cover, and 1 to 2 inches of new snow produced areas of blowing snow with local visibilities below a quarter mile. A peak wind gust of 56 mph was recorded in the northwestern part of Jerauld County, 15 miles northwest of Wessington Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northwest winds, gusting to over 50 mph, combined with existing loose snow cover and 1 to 3 inches of new snow to produce blizzard conditions with widespread visibilities near zero over parts of southeast South Dakota during the day and early evening of January 12th. The very low visibilities were widespread where the greatest loose snow cover was located, and more localized in other areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North to northwest winds sustained at 30 to 40 mph with gusts over 50 mph combined with several inches of loose snow cover, along with 1 to 3 inches of new snow, to produce widespread visibilities of a quarter mile or less in blowing snow. The low visibilities and drifting snow made travel impossible in many areas, and forced schools and businesses to close. A peak wind gust of 56 mph was recorded at Yankton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold temperatures and moderate northwest winds combined to lower wind chill readings to 35 to 45 below zero at times from the morning of January 14th to early morning on January 15th. The strongest winds were at the start of the event. While winds decreased slowly on the night of January 14th, actual temperatures also dropped, reaching 20 below to 30 below zero near daybreak on January 15th, about which time winds became light and no longer a major factor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate northwest winds and very cold air combined to lower wind chills to 35 to 45 below zero at times. While winds were slowly decreasing during this time period, temperatures were steadily dropping.  The actual temperature dropped to 30 below zero at Huron on the morning of January 15th after the wind had subsided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved across the Northern and Central Plains, bringing heavy snow and very strong winds. The worst part of the storm impacted the area from Christmas morning through the morning of December 26, when snow and wind gusts to 80 mph produced near zero visibilities. Six inches to over two feet of snow fell across the plains, with the heaviest amounts from northwestern South Dakota southward to the Pine Ridge. Drifts of five to ten feet were reported. Many roads were closed or impassable during the storm, incuding Interstate 90, which was closed across the state for over 48 hours. Some power outages were also reported.

A vigorous and fast moving upper level system moved through the region and brought 1 to 5 inches of snow along with very strong northwest winds. The snow combined with winds gusting over 60 mph brought widespread blizzard conditions. Visibilities were frequently below a quarter mile with reports of whiteout conditions. In addition, Arctic air surging in resulted in the temperatures falling over 30 degrees from the early morning to the mid afternoon of the 12th. Travel conditions were difficult, if not, impossible across the area and many schools were closed. ||The high winds in the blizzard also caused some damage across the area. The high winds ripped the secondary roof off the old shop building at the McIntosh school. The winds also blew down a building under construction in Pierre and ripped a part of the steel roof off the grandstand at the Sully County Fairgrounds. Some snowfall amounts included, 2 inches at McLaughlin and Onida; 3 inches at Pollock; 4 inches at Britton, Bryant, and Gettysburg. Some of the highest wind gusts included, 49 mph at Aberdeen; 55 mph at Eagle Butte and Mobridge; 56 mph at Pierre; and 63 mph 13 miles north of Vivian.

After a clipper system dropped from 1 to 4 inches of snow on the 13th, Arctic air and blustery north winds pushed into the area.  The coldest air and the lowest wind chills of the season spread across much of central and northeast South Dakota. Wind chills fell to 35 to 50 degrees below zero late in the evening of the 13th and remained through the 14th and into the mid morning hours of the 15th. Across northeast South Dakota, wind chills were as low as 60 degrees below zero by the morning of the 15th. Many vehicles did not start because of the extreme cold and several schools had delayed starts.||The Arctic high pressure area settled in on the morning of the 15th bringing the coldest temperatures to the region in many years. The combination of a fresh and deep snow pack, clear skies, and light winds allowed temperatures to fall to record levels at many locations on the 15th. Daytime highs remained well below zero across the area. This was one of the coldest days that most areas experienced since the early 1970s. The records were broken by 1 to as much as 7 degrees. Some of the record lows included, -30 degrees at Kennebec; -31 degrees at Sisseton; -32 degrees at Milbank; -33 degrees at Mobridge; -35 degrees at Andover and near Summit; -38 degrees at Eureka; -39 degrees 8 miles north of Columbia and Castlewood; -42 degrees at Aberdeen; and -47 degrees at Pollock. Some near record low temperatures included, -24 degrees at Pierre; -29 degrees at Redfield and Victor; -32 degrees at Roscoe; and -34 degrees at Watertown. In Aberdeen, the low temperature of -42 degrees tied the third coldest temperature ever recorded. The coldest temperature ever recorded in Aberdeen was -46 degrees.

A vigorous and fast moving upper level system moved through the region and brought 1 to 5 inches of snow along with very strong northwest winds. The snow combined with winds gusting over 60 mph brought widespread blizzard conditions. Visibilities were frequently below a quarter mile with reports of whiteout conditions. In addition, Arctic air surging in resulted in the temperatures falling over 30 degrees from the early morning to the mid afternoon of the 12th. Travel conditions were difficult, if not, impossible across the area and many schools were closed. ||The high winds in the blizzard also caused some damage across the area. The high winds ripped the secondary roof off the old shop building at the McIntosh school. The winds also blew down a building under construction in Pierre and ripped a part of the steel roof off the grandstand at the Sully County Fairgrounds. Some snowfall amounts included, 2 inches at McLaughlin and Onida; 3 inches at Pollock; 4 inches at Britton, Bryant, and Gettysburg. Some of the highest wind gusts included, 49 mph at Aberdeen; 55 mph at Eagle Butte and Mobridge; 56 mph at Pierre; and 63 mph 13 miles north of Vivian.

Strong north to northwest winds, gusting to over 50 mph, combined with existing loose snow cover and 1 to 3 inches of new snow to produce blizzard conditions with widespread visibilities near zero over parts of southeast South Dakota during the day and early evening of January 12th. The very low visibilities were widespread where the greatest loose snow cover was located, and more localized in other areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North to northwest winds gusting to around 50 mph, existing snow cover, and 1 to 2 inches of new snow produced areas of blowing snow with local visibilities below a quarter mile. A peak gust of 53 mph was recorded at Mitchell.

Some of the record lows included, -30 degrees at Kennebec; -31 degrees at Sisseton; -32 degrees at Milbank; -33 degrees at Mobridge; -35 degrees at Andover and near Summit; -38 degrees at Eureka; -39 degrees 8 miles north of Columbia and Castlewood; -42 degrees at Aberdeen; and -47 degrees at Pollock. Some near record low temperatures included, -24 degrees at Pierre; -29 degrees at Redfield and Victor; -32 degrees at Roscoe; and -34 degrees at Watertown. In Aberdeen, the low temperature of -42 degrees tied the third coldest temperature ever recorded. The coldest temperature ever recorded in Aberdeen was -46 degrees.

After a clipper system dropped from 1 to 4 inches of snow on the 13th, Arctic air and blustery north winds pushed into the area.  The coldest air and the lowest wind chills of the season spread across much of central and northeast South Dakota. Wind chills fell to 35 to 50 degrees below zero late in the evening of the 13th and remained through the 14th and into the mid morning hours of the 15th. Across northeast South Dakota, wind chills were as low as 60 degrees below zero by the morning of the 15th. Many vehicles did not start because of the extreme cold and several schools had delayed starts.||The Arctic high pressure area settled in on the morning of the 15th bringing the coldest temperatures to the region in many years. A powerful winter storm moved across the Northern and Central Plains, bringing heavy snow and very strong winds. The worst part of the storm impacted the area from Christmas morning through the morning of December 26, when snow and wind gusts to 80 mph produced near zero visibilities. Six inches to over two feet of snow fell across the plains, with the heaviest amounts from northwestern South Dakota southward to the Pine Ridge. Drifts of five to ten feet were reported. Many roads were closed or impassable during the storm, incuding Interstate 90, which was closed across the state for over 48 hours. Some power outages were also reported.

Snowfall of 6 to 10 inches over extreme southeast South Dakota was accompanied by north to northwest winds sustained at 30 to 40 mph, with some gusts over 50 mph. The combination of snow and strong winds created widespread blizzard conditions with visibilities near zero. The blizzard closed schools and businesses and halted travel. The snowfall began on the morning of December 8th. The winds increased during the evening of the 8th, and became strong enough to cause the blizzard after midnight on December 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 2 to 4 inches was accompanied by northwest winds gusting to over 40 mph. This caused areas of visibilities below a half mile in the falling and blowing snow, with drifting snow also slowing travel. Wind chills dropped to around 20 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged snowfall produced heavy accumulations over southeast South Dakota, ranging up to over 20 inches in several areas. The snowfall took place from two days before to the day after Christmas. The snowfall was accompanied by increasing north to northwest winds which caused widespread blizzard conditions on Christmas day and the start of the next day. The blizzard added to the very heavy snowfall made almost all travel impossible, and shut down commerce.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow combined with increasing winds to produce widespread blizzard conditions. The snowfall over 3 days varied from 12 to 20 inches, with a 20 inch report at Huron. North to northwest winds reached sustained speeds over 30 mph and there were gusts over 40 mph. These winds combined with the heavy snowfall to produce widespread visibilities below a quarter mile from Christmas day to the start of the next day. Most roads were blocked and travel was brought to a standstill, as was the limited holiday commerce.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level low pressure area over the Upper Midwest associated with an intense surface low pressure area brought widespread heavy snow along with blizzard conditions to central and northeast South Dakota. The storm was a slow mover and produced several rounds of snow over a three-day period. Total snowfall amounts were from 7 to as much as 25 inches. The heavy snow combined with winds of 25 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph brought widespread blowing and drifting snow with visibilities frequently less than a quarter of a mile.||This blizzard ranked in the top three for the entire state snowfall with a state average of 15.4 inches.  Most of the state received 10 inches of snowfall or more with many locations with 20 inches or more.  Pollock in north central South Dakota set an all-time high three-day snowfall total with 17 inches. ||Prior to the onset of the storm, the Governor declared a state of emergency for South Dakota. Large portions of both Interstates 29 and 90 across South Dakota were closed late on Thursday, December 24th. Both Interstates were closed through Christmas Day and into Saturday, December 26th. There were some stranded holiday travelers due to the road closings along with a few rescues. Most roads were reopened by Sunday morning, December 27th. There were also several vehicle accidents with nothing serious. Several airports were also closed throughout the storm along with a few spotty power outages occurring in Lyman county in central South Dakota. The President declared a disaster area for public assistance for several counties in central and north central South Dakota. Costs were estimated in the several hundreds of thousands of dollars. ||Total snowfall amounts over the three-day period predominantly ranged from 1 to 2 feet. Snowfall amounts with a foot or more included; 12 inches at Mobridge, Eureka, Waubay, and Eagle Butte; 13 inches at Highmore and Miller; 14 inches at Castlewood, Summit, Watertown, Pierre, and Ree Heights; 15 inches at Groton, Gettysburg, Webster, Wilmot, Hayti, and McLaughlin; 16 inches at McIntosh, east of Hayes and east of Hosmer; 17 inches at Timber Lake, Britton, and Pollock; 18 inches near Victor; 20 inches near Keldron; 22 inches at Murdo; 23 inches at Sisseton and 25 inches at Kennebec.

A vigorous, but relatively fast moving winter storm tracked across South Dakota and brought impressive snowfall amounts to northern South Dakota. During part of the storm, snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches an hour were observed. The heaviest snow fell across north central and northeast South Dakota with a few locations picking up 12 to 16 inches of snow. Over central South Dakota, freezing rain and sleet mixed in with the snow limiting accumulations there. In fact, Lyman, Jones, and Stanley counties had significant icing and sleet accumulations. Glaze accumulations were from a quarter to a half inch along with sleet accumulations of up to 3 inches. Ice covered highways and sidewalks made driving and walking treacherous. There were many reports of cars going into the ditch. ||Many area schools were cancelled on the 26th due to the heavy snow and icing. Several flights were also cancelled along with no postal delivery for some rural areas due to 3 to 4 foot drifts. ||Some storm total snowfall amounts included; 8 inches at Miller, Orient, Seneca and Faulkton; 9 inches at Aberdeen, Columbia, Ree Heights; 10 inches at Ipswich, Turton, Webster, and Westport; 11 inches at Onaka; 12 inches at Redfield, Bowdle, and Leola; 13 inches at Sisseton, Mobridge, Roscoe and northeast of Isabel; 14 inches at Eureka, Selby, and northeast of Victor; 15 inches southeast of McIntosh and 16 inches at Pollock and Artas.

The heavy snow combined with winds of 25 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph brought widespread blowing and drifting snow with visibilities frequently less than a quarter of a mile.||This blizzard ranked in the top three for the entire state snowfall with a state average of 15.4 inches.  Most of the state received 10 inches of snowfall or more with many locations with 20 inches or more.  Pollock in north central South Dakota set an all-time high three-day snowfall total with 17 inches. ||Prior to the onset of the storm, the Governor declared a state of emergency for South Dakota. Large portions of both Interstates 29 and 90 across South Dakota were closed late on Thursday, December 24th. Both Interstates were closed through Christmas Day and into Saturday, December 26th. There were some stranded holiday travelers due to the road closings along with a few rescues. Most roads were reopened by Sunday morning, December 27th. There were also several vehicle accidents with nothing serious. Several airports were also closed throughout the storm along with a few spotty power outages occurring in Lyman county in central South Dakota. The President declared a disaster area for public assistance for several counties in central and north central South Dakota. Costs were estimated in the several hundreds of thousands of dollars. ||Total snowfall amounts over the three-day period predominantly ranged from 1 to 2 feet. Snowfall amounts with a foot or more included; 12 inches at Mobridge, Eureka, Waubay, and Eagle Butte; 13 inches at Highmore and Miller; 14 inches at Castlewood, Summit, Watertown, Pierre, and Ree Heights; 15 inches at Groton, Gettysburg, Webster, Wilmot, Hayti, and McLaughlin; 16 inches at McIntosh, east of Hayes and east of Hosmer; 17 inches at Timber Lake, Britton, and Pollock; 18 inches near Victor; 20 inches near Keldron; 22 inches at Murdo; 23 inches at Sisseton and 25 inches at Kennebec.

A vigorous, but relatively fast moving winter storm tracked across South Dakota and brought impressive snowfall amounts to northern South Dakota. During part of the storm, snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches an hour were observed. The heaviest snow fell across north central and northeast South Dakota with a few locations picking up 12 to 16 inches of snow. Over central South Dakota, freezing rain and sleet mixed in with the snow limiting accumulations there. In fact, Lyman, Jones, and Stanley counties had significant icing and sleet accumulations. Glaze accumulations were from a quarter to a half inch along with sleet accumulations of up to 3 inches. Ice covered highways and sidewalks made driving and walking treacherous. There were many reports of cars going into the ditch. ||Many area schools were cancelled on the 26th due to the heavy snow and icing. Several flights were also cancelled along with no postal delivery for some rural areas due to 3 to 4 foot drifts. ||Some storm total snowfall amounts included; 8 inches at Miller, Orient, Seneca and Faulkton; 9 inches at Aberdeen, Columbia, Ree Heights; 10 inches at Ipswich, Turton, Webster, and Westport; 11 inches at Onaka; 12 inches at Redfield, Bowdle, and Leola; 13 inches at Sisseton, Mobridge, Roscoe and northeast of Isabel; 14 inches at Eureka, Selby, and northeast of Victor; 15 inches southeast of McIntosh and 16 inches at Pollock and Artas.

A widespread wintry mix of precipitation moved across southeast South Dakota on February 26th; mainly during the daytime hours. Snowfall of 2 to 5 inches was reported north of Interstate 90, while south of the Interstate, precipitation was mostly freezing rain and sleet. Near the Interstate, a mixture fell. The precipitation made driving and walking surfaces slippery.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 2 to 4 inches. The snow was mixed with freezing rain and sleet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread wintry mix of precipitation moved across southeast South Dakota on February 26th; mainly during the daytime hours. Snowfall of 2 to 5 inches was reported north of Interstate 90, while south of the Interstate, precipitation was mostly freezing rain and sleet. Near the Interstate, a mixture fell. The precipitation made driving and walking surfaces slippery.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain was accompanied by snow and sleet, with the snow accumulation around 1 inch. A few vehicles slid into ditches on Interstate 90.

Snowmelt related rises on the Big Sioux River caused moderate flooding in the Bruce to Brookings area from March 17th to the end of the month. Parkland and several county roads were flooded, as well as agricultural land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding along the Big Sioux River resulted from the melting of snow. Farmland, parkland, and several county roads were flooded. The highest reading from the Bruce gage was 2.8 feet above flood stage on March 24th, and the highest reading from the Brookings gage was 1.8 feet above flood stage on March 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Melting snow caused the James River to rise above flood stage from north of Huron to near Mitchell from March 19th through the end of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding along the James River resulted from the melting of snow. Farmland and several county roads near the river were flooded. The highest reading from the Huron gage was 3.3 feet above flood stage, recorded on March 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow accompanied by winds gusting to over 40 mph produced blizzard conditions over southeast South Dakota away from the eastern border of the state. The blizzard began on the evening of March 30th and continued through the daytime hours of March 31st. Travel was brought to a standstill, with numerous roads closed, including Interstate 90 from Mitchell west. The greatest reported snow accumulation was 16.5 inches near Corsica.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 1 to 2 inches over the western part of the county was accompanied by northerly winds gusting to over 40 mph, producing blowing snow with visibilities lowering to around a quarter mile in places.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains on top of deep snow cover caused the flooding of numerous roads across eastern Dewey and eastern Corson counties. Rainfall amounts were anywhere from a half inch to as much as 2 inches.

Another upper level trough brought heavy snow of 6 to 7 inches to parts of central South Dakota which affected travel. Snowfall amounts included; 6 inches at Murdo, Pierre, and Wendte; 7 inches at Kennebec and 6 miles east of Hayes.

Glaze accumulations were from a quarter to a half inch along with sleet accumulations of up to 3 inches. Ice covered highways and sidewalks made driving and walking treacherous. There were many reports of cars going into the ditch. ||Many area schools were cancelled on the 26th due to the heavy snow and icing. Melting snow caused the James River to rise above flood stage from north of Huron to near Mitchell from March 19th through the end of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding along the James River resulted from the melting of snow. Farmland and several county roads near the river were flooded. The highest reading at the Forestburg gage was 2.8 feet above flood stage, recorded at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous and fast moving upper level system moved through the region and brought 1 to 5 inches of snow along with very strong northwest winds. The snow combined with winds gusting over 60 mph brought widespread blizzard conditions. Visibilities were frequently below a quarter mile with reports of whiteout conditions. In addition, Arctic air surging in resulted in the temperatures falling over 30 degrees from the early morning to the mid afternoon of the 12th. Travel conditions were difficult, if not, impossible across the area and many schools were closed. ||The high winds in the blizzard also caused some damage across the area. The high winds ripped the secondary roof off the old shop building at the McIntosh school. The winds also blew down a building under construction in Pierre and ripped a part of the steel roof off the grandstand at the Sully County Fairgrounds. Some snowfall amounts included, 2 inches at McLaughlin and Onida; 3 inches at Pollock; 4 inches at Britton, Bryant, and Gettysburg. Some of the highest wind gusts included, 49 mph at Aberdeen; 55 mph at Eagle Butte and Mobridge; 56 mph at Pierre; and 63 mph 13 miles north of Vivian.

After a clipper system dropped from 1 to 4 inches of snow on the 13th, Arctic air and blustery north winds pushed into the area.  The coldest air and the lowest wind chills of the season spread across much of central and northeast South Dakota. Wind chills fell to 35 to 50 degrees below zero late in the evening of the 13th and remained through the 14th and into the mid morning hours of the 15th. Across northeast South Dakota, wind chills were as low as 60 degrees below zero by the morning of the 15th. Many vehicles did not start because of the extreme cold and several schools had delayed starts.||The Arctic high pressure area settled in on the morning of the 15th bringing the coldest temperatures to the region in many years. The combination of a fresh and deep snow pack, clear skies, and light winds allowed temperatures to fall to record levels at many locations on the 15th. Daytime highs remained well below zero across the area. This was one of the coldest days that most areas experienced since the early 1970s. The records were broken by 1 to as much as 7 degrees. Some of the record lows included, -30 degrees at Kennebec; -31 degrees at Sisseton; -32 degrees at Milbank; -33 degrees at Mobridge; -35 degrees at Andover and near Summit; -38 degrees at Eureka; -39 degrees 8 miles north of Columbia and Castlewood; -42 degrees at Aberdeen; and -47 degrees at Pollock. Some near record low temperatures included, -24 degrees at Pierre; -29 degrees at Redfield and Victor; -32 degrees at Roscoe; and -34 degrees at Watertown. In Aberdeen, the low temperature of -42 degrees tied the third coldest temperature ever recorded. The coldest temperature ever recorded in Aberdeen was -46 degrees.

A major winter storm moved across the Northern Rockies and into the Northern Plains producing from 2 to 22 inches of snowfall along with widespread blizzard conditions. Most area schools and events were canceled. Travel was extremely difficult and not advised. Interstate 29 from Watertown to the North Dakota line and Interstate 90 across Jones and Lyman counties were both closed during the storm. There were several vehicle accidents with no serious injuries reported. However, this storm took a toll on area ranchers as calving season was underway.  Storm total snowfall amounts included; 6 inches in Blunt, Timber Lake, Gettysburg, and Wilmot; 7 inches in Doland and Pierre; 8 inches in Clark, Clear Lake, Leola, Hosmer, Gettysburg, southeast of McIntosh, and Kennebec; 9 inches south of Bristol, Waubay, and near Chelsea; 10 inches in Eagle Butte and Mobridge; and 11 inches in Pollock and Turton. Locations with a foot or more of snowfall included; 12 inches in Aberdeen, Britton, Andover, Sisseton, and Faulkton; 13 inches at Lake Sharpe, Roy Lake, and eight miles southwest of Keldron; 14 inches in Miller, Redfield, and Webster; 15 inches near Highmore and near Columbia; 16 inches southwest of Stratford; 17 inches 14 miles northeast of Isabel; 20 inches in McLaughlin, Ree Heights, and 4 miles northeast of Victor with almost 22 inches northwest of Stephan.

Snowmelt and ice jamming caused the Maple River to flood in northwest Brown County. The Maple River flooded some homes in the town of Frederick along with many roads and pasture and cropland.

A strong low pressure system brought widespread precipitation to the Northern Plains. Temperatures became cold enough over northwestern South Dakota for snow, and two to six inches of snow fell across Perkins and northern Ziebach County. Wind gusts to 50 mph produced blowing snow and visibilities below a quarter of a mile for several hours.

Snowmelt flooding and ice jamming caused the Big Sioux River near Castlewood to crest above flood stage a couple of times from March 19th to March 25th. The river rose only slightly above the flood stage of 11 feet to 11.4 feet. Some agricultural land was flooded as a result.

A strong cold front and upper level system moved through the Northern Plains, bringing heavy snow and strong winds to much of northwestern South Dakota. The heaviest snow fell across Harding, Perkins, and northern Ziebach Counties; where six to 15 inches of snow and blowing and drifting snow were reported. Less snow fell across west central South Dakota, but strong winds produced very low visibilities. Freezing rain also fell across Haakon and eastern Pennington Counties, with ice accumulation as much as a quarter of an inch.

A strong cold front and upper level system moved through the Northern Plains, bringing heavy snow and strong winds to much of northwestern South Dakota. The heaviest snow fell across Harding, Perkins, and northern Ziebach Counties; where six to 15 inches of snow and blowing and drifting snow were reported. Less snow fell across west central South Dakota, but strong winds produced very low visibilities. Freezing rain also fell across Haakon and eastern Pennington Counties, with ice accumulation as much as a quarter of an inch.

Ice jams caused minor flooding along Indian Creek, the South Fork of the Moreau River, and the Belle Fourche River near the diversion canal. Water from Indian Creek flowed over Arpan Road, several yards in Belle Fourche were flooded, and flooded pasture land along the Moreau River affected calving operations.
Ice jams and melting snow caused minor flooding in northwestern South Dakota. Several roads and culverts were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Little Missouri River. The river gauge at Camp Crook crested at 14.84 feet, which is 2.84 ft above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ice jams and melting snow caused minor flooding in northwestern South Dakota. Several roads and culverts were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Moreau River. The river gauge northwest of Faith crested at 16.37 ft, which is 0.37 ft above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful spring storm brought rain, snow, and very strong winds to western South Dakota. Precipitation started as rain, then changed to snow, and blizzard conditions developed. The heaviest snow fell over the northern Black Hills, where 18 to 48 inches of snow was measured. Ten to 20 inches of snow fell across far northwestern South Dakota, with drifts as high as ten feet. Most other locations received at least six inches of snow. Sustained winds of 30 to 55 mph, with gusts over 80 mph, were reported. Interstate 90 and other highways were closed for more than 24 hours. Some power outages were reported, mainly across the northern Black Hills and northwestern South Dakota. Tens of thousands of livestock perished.

The second strong spring storm in a week brought heavy snow and gusty winds to the Black Hills and southwestern South Dakota plains. Snowfall across the plains and southern Black Hills was in the six to 12 inch range. The northern Black Hills received one to two feet of snow, with higher amounts in the highest elevations. Strong wind gusts produced blowing and drifting snow, with visibilities below a quarter of a mile. Many highways were closed for a prolonged time.

The second blizzard in a week brought heavy snow and strong winds to the western South Dakota plains. Northwest wind gusts to 50 mph produced significant blowing and drifting snow, reducing visibilities to near zero for many hours. Reports of six to 12 inches of snow were common, with higher amounts across northwestern South Dakota. Interstate 90 and many other highways were closed for a considerable period of time. More livestock losses occurred during the peak of calving and lambing season.

A vigorous, but relatively fast moving winter storm tracked across South Dakota and brought impressive snowfall amounts to northern South Dakota. During part of the storm, snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches an hour were observed. The heaviest snow fell across north central and northeast South Dakota with a few locations picking up 12 to 16 inches of snow. Over central South Dakota, freezing rain and sleet mixed in with the snow limiting accumulations there. In fact, Lyman, Jones, and Stanley counties had significant icing and sleet accumulations. Glaze accumulations were from a quarter to a half inch along with sleet accumulations of up to 3 inches. Ice covered highways and sidewalks made driving and walking treacherous. There were many reports of cars going into the ditch. ||Many area schools were cancelled on the 26th due to the heavy snow and icing. Several flights were also cancelled along with no postal delivery for some rural areas due to 3 to 4 foot drifts. ||Some storm total snowfall amounts included; 8 inches at Miller, Orient, Seneca and Faulkton; 9 inches at Aberdeen, Columbia, Ree Heights; 10 inches at Ipswich, Turton, Webster, and Westport; 11 inches at Onaka; 12 inches at Redfield, Bowdle, and Leola; 13 inches at Sisseton, Mobridge, Roscoe and northeast of Isabel; 14 inches at Eureka, Selby, and northeast of Victor; 15 inches southeast of McIntosh and 16 inches at Pollock and Artas.

An upper level storm system moved from the Rockies into the Northern Plains, bringing snow and gusty winds to the area. The heaviest snow fell across northwestern South Dakota and the northern Black Hills. Snowfall totals ranged from six to 11 inches. Gusty northwest winds caused blowing and drifting snow across the plains.

Heavy snow accompanied by winds gusting to over 40 mph produced blizzard conditions over southeast South Dakota away from the eastern border of the state. The blizzard began on the evening of March 30th and continued through the daytime hours of March 31st. Travel was brought to a standstill, with numerous roads closed, including Interstate 90 from Mitchell west. The greatest reported snow accumulation was 16.5 inches near Corsica.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 to 9 inches, accompanied by northerly winds gusting over 45 mph, produced blizzard conditions that brought travel and commerce to a standstill. Numerous businesses, schools, and roads were closed; and many roads not officially closed were impassable. New snow accumulation of 8 inches was reported from the town of Woonsocket.

Numerous businesses, schools, and roads were closed, including Interstate 90; and many roads not officially closed were impassable. The city of Mitchell reported 12.5 inches of new snow, and a peak wind gust of 44 mph from the north.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved out of the Rockies and into the Northern Plains producing snow and widespread blizzard conditions across central and north central South Dakota. Winds gusting to over 60 mph along with several inches of snow caused hazardous travel conditions. Interstate 90 was closed for a time across much of Jones and part of Lyman County. Power was also out in parts of Pierre and Mobridge for a short period of time. A strong low pressure system tracking across the panhandle of Oklahoma into the Great Lakes region produced moderate to heavy snow across northeast South Dakota from the morning to the evening of the 10th. Strong north to northwest winds gusting to 45 mph resulted in blizzard conditions. Travel became difficult, if not, impossible across northeast South Dakota. Interstate 29 between Watertown and the North Dakota border was closed for several hours. Several minor accidents occurred along with some injuries. Snowfall amounts included; 3 inches near Milbank; 4 inches near Columbia, Summit, and at Sisseton; 5 inches at Waubay and Wilmot; 6 inches 10 miles northeast of Sisseton, Britton, and Roy Lake; 7 inches in Webster and Westport.||Bitter cold air filtered in behind the low pressure system bringing record cold to the area. On the 11th, Aberdeen and Sisseton broke their record low highs for the date with afternoon highs only reaching zero. The record at Aberdeen had been in place since 1896. Sisseton also set a record low of 14 degrees below zero on March 12th.

A major winter storm moved across the Northern Rockies and into the Northern Plains producing from 2 to 22 inches of snowfall along with widespread blizzard conditions. Most area schools and events were canceled. Travel was extremely difficult and not advised. Interstate 29 from Watertown to the North Dakota line and Interstate 90 across Jones and Lyman counties were both closed during the storm. There were several vehicle accidents with no serious injuries reported. However, this storm took a toll on area ranchers as calving season was underway.  Storm total snowfall amounts included; 6 inches in Blunt, Timber Lake, Gettysburg, and Wilmot; 7 inches in Doland and Pierre; 8 inches in Clark, Clear Lake, Leola, Hosmer, Gettysburg, southeast of McIntosh, and Kennebec; 9 inches south of Bristol, Waubay, and near Chelsea; 10 inches in Eagle Butte and Mobridge; and 11 inches in Pollock and Turton. Locations with a foot or more of snowfall included; 12 inches in Aberdeen, Britton, Andover, Sisseton, and Faulkton; 13 inches at Lake Sharpe, Roy Lake, and eight miles southwest of Keldron; 14 inches in Miller, Redfield, and Webster; 15 inches near Highmore and near Columbia; 16 inches southwest of Stratford; 17 inches 14 miles northeast of Isabel; 20 inches in McLaughlin, Ree Heights, and 4 miles northeast of Victor with almost 22 inches northwest of Stephan.

Rapid snowmelt caused Oak Creek to flood at Wakpala. The creek went above flood stage on March 20th and rose to a major flood stage of 19.3 feet on March 23rd. This stage was just a half of a foot from the record stage in 1997. The city of Wakpala was evacuated with several homes on the east side being flooded. The Red Cross set up shelter in the Wakpala Community Center.

Snowmelt and ice jamming caused minor flooding on the Big Sioux River northwest of Watertown. The Big Sioux River rose above and then fell below flood stage a couple times at gaging locations 3 miles northwest and 7 miles northwest of Watertown. The river first rose above flood stage 3 miles northwest of Watertown on March 16th and on March 17th for 7 miles northwest of Watertown. The Big Sioux River went below flood stage at both locations by March 29th. The flood stages are 8 and 10 feet for the locations northwest of Watertown, respectively. The flooding was minor as the river rose only a tenth of a foot to almost 2 feet above flood stage. Some agricultural land along with few roads were flooded.

Rapid snowmelt and ice jamming caused the Elm River near Westport to rise above flood stage on March 20th. The Elm River reached an all time record level of 22.69 feet on March 25th almost 9 feet above flood stage. The previous record was 22.11 feet set on Apri1 10th, 1969. The flood stage for the Elm River at Westport is 14 feet. The city of Westport was evacuated with the flood waters causing damage to many homes and roads in and around Westport. Also, many other roads and agricultural and pastureland along the river were flooded.  The Elm River slowly receded and fell below flood stage on March 30th. ||The flood waters from the Elm River flowed south and into the northern portion of Moccasin Creek. Subsequently, the Moccasin Creek rose as the water flowed south into the city of Aberdeen. Flooding became a concern for Aberdeen and for areas along the creek north of Aberdeen. The Governor signed an emergency declaration which allowed the state to help with flood response efforts, including sending 50,000 sandbags to the area. Also, the National Guard was activated to move a variety of heavy equipment. Some sandbagging and a falling Elm River kept the Moccasin Creek from causing any significant flooding in and north of Aberdeen. Although, some township and county roads were flooded from the creek.

Rapid snowmelt and ice jamming caused the Grand River at Little Eagle to rise above flood stage on March 20th. The Grand River rose to a record stage of 23.86 feet on March 23rd before falling back to below flood stage on March 25th. The previous record stage was 22.75 feet set back in 1950. Flood stage at Little Eagle is 15 feet. Pastureland along with a few roads were flooded.

Rapid snowmelt and ice jamming caused the Moreau River near Whitehorse to go above flood stage on March 17th. The river rose to a crest of 24.9 feet on March 18th and then slowly fell to below flood stage on March 24th. Flood stage near Whitehorse is 21 feet. Many acres of pastureland were flooded as a result. There were also some evacuations from Whitehorse.

Thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds, and a tornado in southeast South Dakota south of Interstate 90 during the late afternoon of March 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado destroyed a garage and machine shed. A vehicle in the garage was damaged. Debris and equipment from the machine shed was scattered about the farm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow accompanied by winds gusting to over 40 mph produced blizzard conditions over southeast South Dakota away from the eastern border of the state. The blizzard began on the evening of March 30th and continued through the daytime hours of March 31st. Travel was brought to a standstill, with numerous roads closed, including Interstate 90 from Mitchell west. The greatest reported snow accumulation was 16.5 inches near Corsica.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 2 to 8 inches, accompanied by northerly winds gusting over 45 mph, produced blizzard conditions that brought travel and commerce to a standstill. Numerous businesses, schools, and roads were closed; and many roads not officially closed were impassable. The heavier snow accumulations were in the western part of the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 1 to 3 inches was accompanied by northerly winds gusting to over 40 mph, producing blowing snow with visibilities lowering to around a quarter mile in places.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved across the Central Plains producing widespread snow over central and north central South Dakota. Along with the snow came strong north winds of 15 to 30 mph causing areas of blowing snow and poor visibilities. The snow and poor visibility caused some travel problems. Snowfall amounts ranged from a few inches, to almost a foot of snow. March flooding of the James River continued from Columbia to Ashton throughout April. The James River went above flood stage at Redfield on April 18th and continued through the end of the month. The James River at Redfield rose to 25.7 feet on April 30th, almost 6 feet above the flood stage of 20 feet. The James River from Columbia to Ashton was from 6 to as much as 10 feet above flood stage throughout the month. ||The James River continued to cause major issues throughout Brown and Spink counties for roads, fields, cropland, along with some homes. State Highway 34 was closed for about two weeks at the state border near Hecla. The flooding washed away the highway base. The James River west of Hecla became a 3 mile wide lake. Some people near Hecla said this was the highest the James River had been near Hecla in several decades. The high water forced the evacuation of people from two homes near Hecla. Many roads along the James River throughout Brown and Spink counties were closed. Also, several bridges along the river were overtopped. Many outbuildings along the river were flooded and damaged with over 100 livestock deaths attributed to the flooding. ||At the Sand Lake National Wildlife Refuge, both the Sand and Mud Lakes hit record levels on the morning of April 17th. Sand Lake's elevation was 1,292.58 feet, breaking the previous record of 1,292.39 feet in 1997. Mud Lake's elevation was 1,293.36 feet, breaking the previous record of 1,293.29 feet.

Snowmelt caused the flooding of many creeks, streams, roads along with crop and pastureland throughout much of Corson County for the middle of April. The main issues with the flooding were with Oak Creek near Wakpala and McLaughlin. The flooded roads caused many travel problems throughout the county.

Additional snowmelt runoff caused the Moreau River at Whitehorse to rise above flood stage on April 13th. The Moreau rose above the flood stage of 21 feet to nearly 25 feet on April 17th before falling back below flood stage on April 18th. Agricultural and pastureland along with a few roads were flooded.

Additional snowmelt runoff caused the Grand River near Little Eagle to rise above the flood stage of 15 feet on April 12th. The river rose to around 19.5 feet on April 14th before falling back below flood stage on April 16th. Some agricultural and pastureland were flooded along with a few roads.

Additional snowmelt brought the Elm River again above flood stage on April 13th. The Elm River rose above the flood stage of 14 feet to 17.4 feet on April 15th before falling back to below flood stage on April 18th. Several roads along with many acres of cropland and pastureland were flooded.

Heavy snow was accompanied by winds gusting to around 40 mph from the north to northeast. The combination produced frequent visibilities of a quarter mile or less, while the snow accumulation and drifting made travel difficult to impossible. The snow accumulated up to 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulating 5 to 8 inches was accompanied by north to northeast winds gusting to around 40 mph. The combination resulted in visibilities frequently lowering to a quarter mile or less and, along with drifting snow, made travel difficult to impossible. Power outages were reported from snow weighting down power lines, and snow laden trees falling on lines.

Flooding along the James River from heavy rain and snowmelt continued through the month of April. The flooding spread downstream from the Mitchell area to part of the lower James River which was not above flood stage at the end of March. Flooding along the river was major, causing closure of some roads, and affecting pastureland, farmland, and some neighborhoods.

Flooding along the James River, which had been slowly decreasing, increased slightly again as a result of additional heavy rains. No major new problems were reported, but flooding of farmland and pastureland near the river continued, as did flooding of a few low lying roads. The river resumed a slow fall by late July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding along the James River continued from June. The river fell slowly at the start and end of the month, but rose for a while in mid and late July due to heavy rains. The flooding continued to affect farmland and pastureland near the river, with flooding of some low lying roads also continuing. The river crested at 0.7 foot above flood stage at Huron on July 25th.

A long-lived supercell thunderstorm moved southeastward from eastern Pennington County to Todd County and into north central Nebraska. A supercell thunderstorm developed across the northern Black Hills and moved eastward across the Sturgis area, southern Meade County, northeastern Pennington County, Haakon County, and northeastern Jackson County. The storm produced baseball sized near Sturgis, then strong winds and hail larger than baseball sized developed as the storm moved across the plains. There were reports of several boats breaking free of their mooring and floating toward the spillway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived supercell thunderstorm moved southeastward from eastern Pennington County to Todd County and into north central Nebraska. The storm produced large hail, strong wind gusts, numerous funnel clouds, and a brief tornado near Parmalee. Damage along the storm's path included downed trees and minor damage to siding, roofs, and vehicles.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down west of Dupree and tracked eastward before dissipating northeast of Dupree. It dented several grain bins, blew over a small mobile home and semi trailer truck, tore sheet metal off sheds, and toppled a large communications tower.
